cards.title: Warm vs. Cold Audiences
cards.front: What's the one mental model that divides ALL of your Meta targeting into two buckets?
cards.back:
Warm vs. Cold Audiences:
- WARM = people who already had contact with your business (fans, website visitors, ad/page engagers, video viewers, CRM lists, pixel hits). Built from your own data. Principle: it's cheaper to keep an existing customer than attract a new one. Also the seed for finding cold audiences via lookalikes.
- COLD = people unfamiliar with you (the vast majority of Facebook). Vital for scaling, but never show your message to just anyone.
You must target BOTH — there's a synergy/feedback loop: warm makes you better at cold (lookalike source), cold grows your warm. Start collecting your own Facebook data ASAP.

cards.title: Two Sources of Warm Audiences
cards.front: Where do warm audiences actually come from on Facebook?
cards.back:
Two sources of warm audiences:
- Fans — automatically stored as a targetable audience, nothing extra needed.
- Custom Audiences — four types by source: (1) Contact Lists (leads/customers), (2) Engagement on Facebook, (3) Website Visitors, (4) Mobile App Engagement.
Limit: up to 500 custom audiences per ad account.

cards.title: Two Ways to Find Cold Audiences
cards.front: What are the two ways to reach people who've never heard of you?
cards.back:
Two ways to find cold audiences:
- Leverage Facebook's own user data — location, demographics, interests, behaviors, offline spending (interest research).
- Most powerful: combine YOUR data with Facebook's to create lookalikes.

cards.title: Don't Chase Cheap/Tricked Likes
cards.front: Why can bait like "Click Like if you love being in shape" actually hurt you?
cards.back: Don't chase cheap/tricked likes. Bait such as "Click Like if you love being in shape" or "Click Like if you Love Making Money" loads you with poor-quality leads. Warm-audience ROI only works with QUALITY fans who genuinely want to hear from you.

cards.title: Never Scrape Data
cards.front: What single data-collection shortcut can violate Facebook Policy and get your account in trouble?
cards.back: Never scrape data. Always have permission to collect info before uploading — scraping violates Facebook Policy. When you upload a customer phone/email list, Facebook recognizes ~half (~50%) of it.

cards.title: More Data Fields = Better Match Rate
cards.front: Why is uploading just an email list leaving match rate on the table?
cards.back: More data fields = better match rate. Submit first name, last name, email, phone, city, zip — not email alone. Uploadable contact-list fields include: email, phone, first name, last name, city, state/province, country, date of birth, year of birth, age, zip/postal code, gender, mobile advertiser ID, Facebook ID. A list typically matches ~50%.

cards.title: UPSYD Awareness/Intent Ladder
cards.front: How do you match your message to where someone sits on the awareness/intent spectrum?
cards.back: UPSYD audience awareness/intent ladder — match message to audience by level of awareness, intent, and trust. Progression: Unaware of the problem → Problem Aware (intent to solve) → Solution Aware → intent to buy YOUR specific product. The book centers on five critical audience awareness levels and stages of intent. Moving targeting UP the UPSYD ladder (e.g., from broad "guns" to "owns specific gun brands") often performs better while staying large enough.
cards.example: Broad "guns" interest vs. up-the-ladder "owns specific gun brands."

cards.title: The Six Brainstorming Triggers
cards.front: Stuck for interests to target? What six prompts unlock a full list?
cards.back:
The Six Brainstorming Triggers:
- Competitors
- Leaders and gurus in your market
- Tools / Equipment / Services
- Publications (blogs, TV shows, books, magazines, podcasts)
- Organizations your avatar might belong to
- Other Behavior (ideas that fit no category)

cards.title: BCOP — Audience Research Process
cards.front: What 4-step process turns a blank spreadsheet into a shortlist of winning interests?
cards.back:
BCOP (Brainstorm, Confirm, Organize, Prioritize) — for ecommerce/digital/info products (NOT local, which use geo). Start with ~20 interests for large budgets, ~5 for small.
- Brainstorm: know avatar, hook/offer, and UPSYD segment first; use the Six Brainstorming Triggers + Audience Insights Page Likes.
- Confirm: verify each interest is actually targetable in Power Editor; record potential reach.
- Organize & Combine: sort by theme, largest→smallest reach; interests >3M go to "Large Audiences" → use Narrow to combine into the 400K–2M sweet spot.
- Prioritize by Avatar Match: (1) Does it sound like my avatar (incl. UPSYD position)? (2) Does a LARGE PERCENTAGE sound like my avatar?

cards.title: Audience Insights ≠ Targetable
cards.front: You found a perfect interest in Audience Insights — why might you still not be able to target it?
cards.back: Audience Insights ≠ targetable. An interest shown in Audience Insights may not exist in Power Editor — always confirm in Power Editor (this is BCOP Step 2: Confirm). Record each interest's potential reach there. You can skip if you used the Power Editor Suggestion Tool and already recorded reaches.

cards.title: Don't Pile All Interests Into One Ad Set
cards.front: Why does dumping every suggested interest into one ad set sabotage your optimization?
cards.back: Don't pile all suggested interests into one ad set — separate interests into separate ad sets so you can compare response per interest. Piling them together loses the ability to compare per-interest performance.

cards.title: OR vs. AND Targeting (Narrowing)
cards.front: Why does stacking interests in one include box quietly broaden your audience instead of tightening it?
cards.back:
Detailed targeting logic:
- Stacking criteria in ONE "include" box = OR targeting (broad — meets at least one). This is the "OR-by-accident" trap.
- Use audience NARROWING for AND targeting (must meet ALL).
cards.example: AND example: homeowner AND likes cooking AND parent.

cards.title: The Reach Sweet Spot
cards.front: What potential-reach range should a single interest-based ad set land in?
cards.back:
Potential reach sweet spot:
- Including the U.S.: 400,000–2,000,000
- Smaller-population countries (U.S. not included): 100,000–500,000
Any interest with reach >3,000,000 = "Large Audience" → use the Narrow feature in Power Editor to combine down into 400K–2M. Power Editor Suggestion Tool: record suggested interests with reach >100,000.

cards.title: Website Custom Audience — Rule Options
cards.front: What rules can you use to slice website visitors into a custom audience?
cards.back:
Website Custom Audience rule options (up to 5: 1 inclusive + up to 4 more):
- All Website Visitors (everyone in last ≤180 days)
- URL Contains / URL Doesn't Contain / URL Equals
- Frequency (visited a particular number of times)
- Device (visited using a specific device)
- Visitors by Time Spent (those who spent the most time)
Pixel Event Rules: URL · Frequency · Device.

cards.title: Engagement Custom Audiences Are Dynamic
cards.front: Why does an engagement custom audience change size on its own over time?
cards.back: Engagement custom audiences are dynamic: people are added as they engage and dropped once their engagement passes the time window. Lookback windows: video/canvas/page engagement up to 365 days; lead-form engagement up to 90 days; website custom audience up to 180 days; app engagement up to 180 days.

cards.title: Abandoned-Cart Custom Audience
cards.front: How do you build an audience of people who added to cart but never bought?
cards.back:
Abandoned-cart audience build:
- Audiences → Create Custom Audience → Website Traffic → Custom Combination
- Switch the dropdown from "URL" to "Event"
- INCLUDE event: AddToCart
- EXCLUDE event: Purchase

cards.title: Combine Custom Audiences in One Ad Set
cards.front: How do you retarget your entire warm audience with a single ad?
cards.back: Combine multiple custom audiences in one ad set to retarget your entire warm audience with a single ad. Note: SAVED audiences can't be combined — only custom audiences can.

cards.title: Hot Audiences & Salience Bias
cards.front: Who is a "hot" audience, and why does showing them the same ad repeatedly work?
cards.back: A HOT audience = people who started but didn't complete the buying process (often just distracted — online attention span ~5 seconds). Without a system to recover them you leave easy money on the table. Repeated retargeting exposure exploits the salience bias: seeing the same ad over and over makes even a small brand feel like "a really big deal."

cards.title: Lookalikes — Highest-Leverage Cold Targeting
cards.front: What's the single highest-leverage way to target cold audiences at scale?
cards.back: Lookalikes: ask Facebook to find the people most like your customers — its algorithm analyzes your source (fans or a custom audience) and matches by demographics, location, interests, behaviors. Larger + more ideal-customer-like source = better lookalike. Facebook recommends a source of 1,000–50,000 people; agencies have seen good results from ~1,000 data points; "fully dialed in" at 60,000–100,000 data points (video views build this fast).

cards.title: Lookalike Rules & Limits
cards.front: What are the hard rules and limits for creating a lookalike audience?
cards.back:
Lookalike rules & limits:
- Country-specific — must name the country/countries.
- Source must contain ≥100 people from the SAME country (those 100 needn't be from the target country — a U.S. source can build a U.K. lookalike).
- Size = 1–10% of population (1% = most similar/smallest, 10% = broadest).
- Up to 500 lookalikes per single source audience.
- Source members are excluded from the lookalike UNLESS the source is a pixel.
- Build: Ads Manager → Audiences → Create Audience → Lookalike Audience → Choose Source → Country → Size → Create. Allow 6–24 hrs to generate.

cards.title: Lookalikes — Step Up the Funnel
cards.front: You don't have 1,000 customers. How do you still build a strong lookalike?
cards.back: If you lack 1,000 customers, step UP the funnel and use leads as the source. Example: 200 customers but 4,000 leads → leads may be the better source. Trade-off: quantity of data vs. quality of data. Test to see which wins.
cards.example: 200 customers vs. 4,000 leads → test leads as the lookalike seed.

cards.title: Customer Lifetime Value Lookalikes
cards.front: How do you make Facebook clone your BEST customers, not just any customer?
cards.back: Customer Lifetime Value lookalikes: upload LTV as an extra data field so Facebook builds lookalikes off your highest-value customers, not just any customer.

cards.title: Video View Audiences — Best Lookalike Seeds
cards.front: What audience type builds a huge, cheap lookalike seed fastest?
cards.back: Video view audiences are among the best lookalike seeds — they build large, cheap data pools fast, giving Facebook lots of data. Video view custom audiences ("quality custom audiences" / "invisible lists") segment by % watched and are often "10x better and 2–3x cheaper" than a website click; gains compound as the algorithm learns who watches.

cards.title: Geographic / Local Targeting
cards.front: Local businesses target by geography, not interests — what are the core geo controls?
cards.back:
Geographic / local targeting (local businesses are primarily geo, rarely interest-based):
- Two methods: type a CITY (radius 10–50 mi) or DROP A PIN (radius 1–50 mi, finer minimum control).
- Geo levels: country, state/province, city, congressional district, zip/postcode, plus worldwide/regions/free-trade-areas/iTunes-store-countries/emerging markets.
- Increase your radius only by building authority/expert status (useful content, branding campaign) and social proof (friends who liked the ad/page) so people will travel to you.

cards.title: Four Location Refinement Modes
cards.front: "Everyone in this location" is the default — what are the four location modes and when do they shine?
cards.back:
Four location refinement modes:
- Everyone in this location (default — currently in area).
- People who live in this location.
- People recently in this location (phone location — powerful for local; hit them near the store).
- People traveling in this location (>125 miles from home — great for travel/tourism).
Hyperlocal proximity: reach visitors traveling within ~10 miles with a real-time offer (e.g., "Come in for 30% off!").

cards.title: Large-Audience Algorithm Play
cards.front: When does targeting a HUGE audience to leverage the algorithm actually work — and when does it fail?
cards.back: Target large audiences to leverage the algorithm: 4–5M, best case 40–50M — but it only works above a conversion-volume threshold: 20–30 conversions/week per ad set minimum (ideally 10–20+/day), using the Website Conversions objective. For smaller-population countries (e.g., Australia), fall back to 100K–500K. Below this conversion floor the large-audience strategy fails.

cards.title: Connection Targeting & Friends of Fans
cards.front: How can 10,000 fans become a ~200,000-person credible audience instantly?
cards.back: Connection types (work with custom/new audiences only — NOT saved audiences): fans of your page, friends of fans, app users, friends of app users, event responders. Friends of Fans grows exponentially: 10K fans → ~200K friends-of-fans audience; growing to 12K fans can lift it to ~230K. Adds instant credibility (appears friend-recommended).

cards.title: Exclusions Protect Ad Spend
cards.front: What targeting setting quietly stops you from paying to reach people you shouldn't?
cards.back:
Exclusions protect ad spend:
- Exclude existing leads/customers on conversion campaigns.
- Exclude your fans to reach a truly cold audience.
- Can also exclude geos, interests, connections.

cards.title: Three Audience Choices When Building an Ad Set
cards.front: When you build an ad set, what three audience options does Facebook give you?
cards.back:
Three audience choices when building an ad set:
- A SAVED audience (interest/demo built earlier).
- A CUSTOM audience (all custom, lookalikes, or just custom).
- Create a NEW audience inline.

cards.title: Saved Audiences Are Limited
cards.front: What surprising restrictions trip people up with Saved Audiences?
cards.back:
Saved audiences are limited:
- Only ONE usable at a time.
- Edits must be made to the ORIGINAL saved audience (not inside the ad set).
- Can't be combined (build a new saved audience to combine).
- Connections must be set when the saved audience is CREATED.

cards.title: Like Campaign Cold-Audience Targeting
cards.front: How do you set up the targeting for a cold-audience Like campaign?
cards.back:
Like Campaign cold-audience ad set targeting:
1. Choose the Facebook page to promote.
2. Target COLD audiences. Geo start: U.S., Canada, U.K., Australia, New Zealand. To reach English speakers elsewhere, set Languages = "English (All)".
3. Set Age & Gender (narrow if niche).
4. Detailed Targeting: pick just ONE or TWO related interests → ~1.5M reach. (Separate interests into separate ad sets.)
5. Connections: "Exclude people who like your page."

cards.title: Influencer & Media Inception Saved Audiences
cards.front: How do you target the people who WRITE the news rather than the people who read it?
cards.back:
Reusable / saved audiences for reach:
- Pre-build a Saved Audience of influencers (magazines, radio, papers, TV, bloggers, conference attendees) so it's one click when boosting.
- Media Inception Audience: target people who WORK AT Forbes / CNN / WSJ (not their readers) so they may write about or share your content; build as a reusable Saved Audience (e.g., "mega media inception").
- Match the audience to each post — the right audience differs per post (e.g., a "hustle"-themed article → Gary Vaynerchuk fans).
cards.example: Hustle-themed article boosted to Gary Vaynerchuk fans.

cards.title: Wrong Audience for Good Content
cards.front: Why can a near-perfect "unicorn" post still completely flop?
cards.back: Wrong audience for good content: a near-"unicorn" post can flop because it was boosted to the wrong audience. The right audience differs per post. Most tuning work is trying audience combinations, not making new posts.

cards.title: Five-Tier Scaling — Tier 2: Audience Scaling
cards.front: Beyond lookalikes, how do you mine Facebook for MORE audiences similar to your winners?
cards.back:
Five-Tier Scaling System — Tier 2: Audience Scaling. Get ads in front of more people similar to WINNING audiences by mining Facebook's data for related interests/behaviors (not only lookalikes). If "Frank Kern" works, "Ryan Deiss" likely works too.
Process (Audience Insights): set calendar last 7 days → sort ad sets by CPA smallest→largest → screen for ad sets with significant conversions + lowest CPA → record their targeting → insert into Audience Insights → choose "Page Likes" → record new relevant interests → confirm in Power Editor + record reach.
The Power Editor Suggestion Tool only lists targetable interests — use both, Suggestion Tool first.

cards.title: Scaling — Duplicate & Insert New Interests
cards.front: After finding 5–20 new winning interests, how do you actually deploy them while keeping social proof?
cards.back:
After identifying 5–20 new relevant, appropriately-sized interests:
- Highlight ad sets to scale and Duplicate.
- Insert new interests into the duplicates (e.g., "Robert Kiyosaki" → 3 duplicates with Tony Robbins, Brendan Burchard, Dave Ramsey).
Housekeeping: rename ad sets to reflect the new interest; verify reach stays 400K–2M; insert the Post ID to keep social proof. If highly relevant audiences miss the 400K–2M requirement, group several similar ones.
cards.example: "Robert Kiyosaki" → duplicates targeting Tony Robbins, Brendan Burchard, Dave Ramsey.

cards.title: Avatar, Ideal Avatar & Sub-Avatars
cards.front: Why is "how to play guitar" a weaker hook than "Eddie Van Halen's Top 10 Licks"?
cards.back:
Identify your avatar AND your IDEAL avatar (most valuable customer = one you'll pay more to acquire; spends more, refers more). Watch for sub-avatars; the more specific the hook to the sub-avatar, the better.
- Too broad: "How to play guitar"
- Better: "Learn to Play Eddie Van Halen's Top 10 Guitar Licks" (hard-rock sub-avatar)
Every audience also contains multiple buyer personas — Impulsive, Analytical, Emotional/humanistic, Competitive (per Kolbe "Fact Finder" scale) — so create varied messaging.
cards.example: Generic "how to play guitar" vs. "Eddie Van Halen's Top 10 Guitar Licks."

cards.title: Content-to-Offer Retargeting
cards.front: How do you retarget visitors with the EXACT offer matching the article they read?
cards.back:
Content-to-offer retargeting: segment visitors by which article/topic they consumed, then retarget each segment with the topically matched offer.
- Article 1 (tennis): "Three Quick Tips to Easily Add 10 MPH to Your Tennis Serve" → "Special Edition Tennis Racquet" offer.
- Article 2 (basketball): vertical-leap article → "Vertical Leap Explosion" basketball program.
cards.example: Tennis content → tennis racquet; vertical-leap content → basketball program.

cards.title: Run Varied Copy to the SAME Audiences
cards.front: Why run both long-copy AND short-copy ads to the very same audience?
cards.back: Run different ad styles (long-copy AND short-copy) to the SAME audiences — each audience holds people at different awareness/intent levels (UPSYD), so varied messaging captures more of them. Also note: in the takeoff (0–100) phase, don't focus on building warm audiences yet — keep it simple with your two starter campaigns (Like ad + Website Conversions link-post ad).

cards.title: Act on Breakdown Data
cards.front: Your cheapest-lead age group isn't your highest-revenue age group — now what?
cards.back: Act on breakdown data: e.g., ages 25–34 gave the cheapest leads while 55–64 gave the most revenue → add new audiences + demographic targeting to maximize budget. Large-metro local option: build a 10% lookalike from your best custom audience, then overlay geo + demographic targeting (needs a large enough population).
cards.example: 25–34 = cheapest leads; 55–64 = most revenue → split into demographic-targeted audiences.
